name: Berries

book_claims.mechanism: Smaller effect on blood glucose due to lower sugar content and higher fibre relative to other fruits

supplementary_research.finding: Systematic review and meta-analysis (Huang et al., 2019) of 27 RCTs found berry consumption significantly reduced LDL cholesterol, systolic blood pressure, fasting glucose, and TNF-alpha inflammatory marker.

Nurses' Health Study (Cassidy et al., 2013) found women consuming 3+ servings of blueberries and strawberries per week had 34% lower risk of heart attack compared to those eating berries once a month or less.
Meta-analysis (Silva et al., 2020) found berry anthocyanin intake associated with 12% lower risk of all-cause mortality in pooled prospective studies.

culinary_pairings.source: Classic breakfast pairing; probiotics plus polyphenols
Classic flavour brightener in berry desserts and salads
Citric acid enhances berry flavour and preserves colour
Fat aids carotenoid absorption; textural contrast
Aged balsamic with strawberries; acidity amplifies berry sweetness

seasonality: Peak season varies by type: strawberries May-July, blueberries June-August, raspberries June-October, blackberries July-September (Northern Hemisphere). Frozen berries retain most nutrients and are available year-round.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Low absorption (~1-2%) but gut microbial metabolites (protocatechuic acid, hippuric acid) are well absorbed and bioactive.
Highly bioavailable from whole fruit. Strawberries provide more vitamin C per 100g than oranges.
Seeds contribute insoluble fibre. Pectin provides soluble fibre fermented to beneficial SCFAs.
Converted by gut bacteria to urolithins with anti-inflammatory and mitophagy-promoting properties.
Better absorbed than resveratrol (80% vs 20% oral bioavailability). Activates SIRT1 and AMPK pathways.
